text:
Welcome - hope this helps ..

Who is your ideal early adopter? ... Why would I use Budibase, rather than say bubble or airtable. ... For people who have already looked at 10 low code apps, what is the key thing that would get them to sign up to yours?

With any website, I normally ask 2 questions. Who is this for, is it for me?.  What is the value add, what makes it better than other available options.

elements.text:
Welcome - hope this helps ..

Who is your ideal early adopter? ... Why would I use Budibase, rather than say bubble or airtable. ... For people who have already looked at 10 low code apps, what is the key thing that would get them to sign up to yours?

With any website, I normally ask 2 questions. Who is this for, is it for me?.  What is the value add, what makes it better than other available options.

text:
Hey Eddy. I love your approach. 

Who is it for:
We're targeting developers and technical entrepreneurs. 

Value add:

- We're open source, and more flexible than other platforms. With Budibase, we take away a lot of the grunt work similar to other platforms, but we also offer users the flexibility to add snippets of code within different areas of the platform (such as events) which we feel makes it more powerful. 

- We're component based, allowing users to choose from material / bootstrap components (and hopefully others in the future). 

- Users can host wherever they please. We're working on releasing the builder and we hope to add a hosting and analytics platform in the following months but we feel users should have a choice when it comes to their data. 

Budibase is closer to Bubble than Airtable. Users can build a complete Web App including their backend, frontend, and State, all within Budibase. I feel Budibase offers a better dev experience for building web apps, and I would love the opportunity to explain this more over a call if you (or anyone else) like?

elements.text:
Hey Eddy. I love your approach. 

Who is it for:
We're targeting developers and technical entrepreneurs. 

Value add:

- We're open source, and more flexible than other platforms. With Budibase, we take away a lot of the grunt work similar to other platforms, but we also offer users the flexibility to add snippets of code within different areas of the platform (such as events) which we feel makes it more powerful. 

- We're component based, allowing users to choose from material / bootstrap components (and hopefully others in the future). 

- Users can host wherever they please. We're working on releasing the builder and we hope to add a hosting and analytics platform in the following months but we feel users should have a choice when it comes to their data. 

Budibase is closer to Bubble than Airtable. Users can build a complete Web App including their backend, frontend, and State, all within Budibase. I feel Budibase offers a better dev experience for building web apps, and I would love the opportunity to explain this more over a call if you (or anyone else) like?

text: Welcome <@UTGL3V81Y>. Looking at your landing page I’d recommend working through Julian’s fantastic guide and applying it: <https://www.julian.com/guide/growth/landing-pages>. I’ve been there as well but in your current form your landing page doesn’t really reveal much to me. Everybody says they’re faster, bigger, better. Julian’s advice is to be specific.

text:
Hey Martin. I love that guide. There are plans to revise the landing page . We've had 5000 signups, and surveyed 1000 of them. We are now hoping to talk with 40 this month. This research and feedback will help determine the content for the page, allowing us to focus on the crucial problems and where we add value.

But you are right, the current landing page is very run of the mill. We've no screenshots on the site which is not very insightful.

text: Thank you for the suggestion <@UP6G25H35> . There is a feature of Scratch that is called a ‘Cloud Variable’. The discoverability is a tricky thing/hard currently. When you create a variable there is a selection to make it cloud based. This allows the creation of persistent high score tables.

elements.text: Thank you for the suggestion

. There is a feature of Scratch that is called a ‘Cloud Variable’. The discoverability is a tricky thing/hard currently. When you create a variable there is a selection to make it cloud based. This allows the creation of persistent high score tables.

text: There is a balance that we have to strike with it in terms of resources, the backend for it is limited to 128 digit numbers, and updated up to 10/s.

elements.text: There is a balance that we have to strike with it in terms of resources, the backend for it is limited to 128 digit numbers, and updated up to 10/s.

text: The limit to 128 digits is to limit ease of creation for things like chat rooms. And we aggressively drop/shape connections to hold to the 10/s because the current design of scratch makes it trivially easy to loop updates to a variable at thousands of times per sec

elements.text: The limit to 128 digits is to limit ease of creation for things like chat rooms. And we aggressively drop/shape connections to hold to the 10/s because the current design of scratch makes it trivially easy to loop updates to a variable at thousands of times per sec

text: With those constraints we still handle between 500-1000Mbps of traffic in cloud variable updates alone

elements.text: With those constraints we still handle between 500-1000Mbps of traffic in cloud variable updates alone

text: There are lots of areas to improve here for our specific implementation and how we design for it in the future

elements.text: There are lots of areas to improve here for our specific implementation and how we design for it in the future

text: Expectations around how the variables are updated/queued for least surprising results and and so on. Discoverability also needs work

text:
Hello, I'm Matthew. I'm a software engineer and very interested in the Future of Programming. I'm here both for my personal interest and my day job, as I work at the firm behind HighGear, which is a no code workflow platform. We enable business analysts to implement sophisticated business process requirements in an ergonomic and powerful visual programming language. I understand that the no code movement has some bad rep in this community, and I (and we) are not threatened by that. I'm excited to learn more about and discuss the future of programming and computing with you all!
<http://highgear.com>

attachments.title: HighGear: The Visual No-Code Workflow Automation Platform
attachments.text: HighGear is the leading, visual no-code workflow automation platform for business analysts to rapidly build enterprise-grade workflow applications.
attachments.fallback: HighGear: HighGear: The Visual No-Code Workflow Automation Platform

elements.text: Hello, I'm Matthew. I'm a software engineer and very interested in the Future of Programming. I understand that the no code movement has some bad rep in this community, and I (and we) are not threatened by that. I'm excited to learn more about and discuss the future of programming and computing with you all!

text:
Hi Matthew! Welcome. I'm excited to learn more about your personal FoC interests and see what the HighGear language is like. Is that the part of HighGear that you work on?

Also, how did you learn about our community? I'm always curious to hear which tendril out into the world leads people back here.

text: Yeah, the first sequence is definitely how it looks whenever I make something creative — and it took me a long time to learn that _this is okay and normal_. When you're writing a song, or making a video, or painting, or sculpting, it's usually going to look like total crap until the last 5% of the process. The trick is to be able to hold some vision or aspiration for the final form in your mind while working on something that is painfully unfinished.

text: Whereas the second sequence is how my finished creative works look through the history of my life. When I first started writing (and finishing) songs and art and software, they were all so _modest_ because I hadn't yet learned that you need the first sequence to go from zero to _massive_.

text:
I don't think privacy concerns are a major issue in post-mortem replay. Given how often people use cloud-based products where all of the customer data is in plain view of the provider's staff, people are not nearly as guarded about their data as they were in previous times.  Also, it would be elective from the user, just like Apple currently does with their crash reporter, where you are given the option to send it to apple.

In mass-market applications, the inability to reproduce client side errors is a huge problem. Adobe, MS, Apple all have millions of unsolved "cannot duplicate" issues in their bug reporting systems. In fact, the inability to fix bugs has made Jira and many other bug-tracking companies huge sums of money. You should be able to count your bugs on your hands! People are way to accepting of sloppy work in software. In Open Source there is this constant churn of changes as people fix 5 bugs and add 2 more, and with feature editions many products never stabilize (like Windows)

text: webreplay is indeed very clever. It is however in its current form a hacked version of Firefox, so it is not available for normal end users to use. It is a great development tool to be sure, and will be popular i predict, but it doesn't tackle the issue of end user bugs, which are the ones that cause so much trouble.

text:
That's a great point. I think there are three milestones that are all important and distinct

1. Can we help developers more productive at debugging
2. Can we help teams be more productive at diagnosing bugs
3. Can we surface issues in production

That's a great point. I think there are three milestones that are all important and distinct

1. Can we help developers more productive at debugging
2. Can we help teams be more productive at diagnosing bugs
3. Can we surface issues in production
Recording in the wild is the end goal, but it'd be easy to get distracted going for that first
Recording in the wild is the end goal, but it'd be easy to get distracted going for that first
The exciting thing is that our architecture is becoming more modular, which will help us in the long run.
